title: runRealDeploy has no child.on('error') handler — spawn ENOENT hangs until timeout
description: The Promise in runRealDeploy only registers 'close' on the child. If spawn fails synchronously (e.g. EACCES) or asynchronously (the most common case on macOS/Linux is ENOENT when pnpm is on PATH at probe time but vanishes by the time the child actually executes — also possible if the spawned binary lacks +x), Node emits an 'error' event on the ChildProcess and never emits 'close'. With no 'error' listener, two things happen: (1) the promise never resolves, and (2) Node may treat the unhandled 'error' as an uncaught exception in some versions. The 7-minute timeoutHandle eventually calls child.kill() on a child that already failed, then close still doesn't fire, so the executor stays pending until the test runner / MCP transport gives up.
impact: Real-mode deploy remediation can hang the entire MCP request for 7 minutes on a misconfigured pnpm, masquerading as a deploy timeout. The student sees 'Deploy timed out after 420000ms' when the actual error was 'pnpm not found'. T-157 is correctly deferred to Phase F today, but Phase F will reproduce this hang under the right environmental conditions.
recommendation: Add `child.on('error', (err) => { clearTimeout(timeoutHandle); resolve({ pass: false, message: `Failed to spawn pnpm deploy-all: ${err.message}`, logs }); });` immediately after the spawn call. Consider also surfacing a dedicated warning kind (e.g. `preflight-deploy-spawn-error`) so the skill can distinguish 'pnpm missing' from 'deploy ran and timed out'.

title: runRealDeploy ignores child exit code — non-zero deploy failure is silently absorbed
description: The close handler signature is `(_code: number | null) => {}` — the underscore prefix and lack of any subsequent reference indicate the exit code is intentionally discarded. After close, the executor unconditionally enters the manifest re-probe loop regardless of whether the deploy actually succeeded. If `pnpm deploy-all --quick` exits with code 1 (e.g. compile failure inside the sandbox), the only signal the student gets is 'manifest still unreachable after 3 attempts' — a downstream symptom, not the actual root cause. The deploy logs are present in the response but the categorical 'this deploy command failed' bit is lost.
impact: Diagnostic clarity: the surfaced warning reads as a manifest reachability problem when the underlying issue is a build/deploy failure. Skill-side messaging will guide the student toward 'pnpm down + retry' when the real fix is 'inspect the deploy logs for the compile error'. Non-trivial debugging cost on E-007-adjacent failures.
recommendation: Capture the exit code, include it in logs (`logs.push(`[deploy-all] exited with code ${code}`)`), and treat any non-zero exit as a distinct failure class — possibly a new `preflight-deploy-exit-nonzero` warning that surfaces the code alongside the captured logs. At minimum stop ignoring the code with `_code`.

title: saveState leaves orphan tmp files on any failure after writeFile — wx flag amplifies the problem
description: saveState writes to `state.tmp-${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}.json` with `{ flag: 'wx' }`, then opens, syncs, closes, and renames. If anything between writeFile-success and rename throws (open rejects, sync rejects, close rejects, rename rejects with EBUSY/EACCES), the tmp file is left behind — there is no try/catch wrapping the durability sequence to unlink the tmp on failure. Combined with cycle 2's archive directory lifecycle, the user's `.sui-deepbook-course/` directory accumulates `state.tmp-*.json` files. Worse, a Math.random() suffix collision with a leftover tmp from a prior crashed run means the subsequent writeFile gets EEXIST and `saveState` permanently rejects — the contract surfaces it (good) but the recovery path requires the user to manually inspect and delete `.sui-deepbook-course/state.tmp-*.json`.
impact: Disk litter under normal operation; permanent saveState failure under collision (rare but real because Math.random() entropy is short and the user might run many save cycles). Cycle 2 carry-forward C016 was supposed to harden the durability path; the wx hardening introduces a new fallback hazard that wasn't present pre-cycle-3.
recommendation: Wrap the open→sync→close→rename block in try/catch that unlinks the tmp file in the catch (best-effort, swallowing only ENOENT). Alternatively, document the expected cleanup behavior and add a startup sweep that removes stale `state.tmp-*` files older than a few minutes. The 'wx' guarantee that the file did not pre-exist needs a counterpart 'we always clean up after ourselves' guarantee.

title: saveState calls fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd) redundantly to satisfy a test ordering spy
description: After `await handle.sync()` (which already calls fdatasync/fsync under the hood), the implementation also runs `fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd)` with the comment 'Redundant but required for T-061's fs.fsync* spy ordering assertion.' This couples production durability code to test-spy mechanics. Concretely: if `handle.sync()` rejects, the finally still runs `handle.close()` (good), but if the redundant `fs.fsyncSync` throws (e.g. EBADF on an unusual filesystem, or after handle.sync fails to actually flush), the saveState rejection masks the original sync error. Errors from the redundant call shadow the more useful errors from the FileHandle path. The comment itself signals 'this is here for the test, not for correctness' — that's a maintenance smell.
impact: Diagnostic clarity loss when sync genuinely fails on disk. Future maintainers seeing the redundant call may delete it (correctly) and break T-061. Conversely they may add a third fsync to satisfy a future spy. The cycle 2 carry-forward fix is real (the FileHandle is now from an 'r+' open, which addresses C002+C027) but the rewrite is half-done.
recommendation: Update T-061 in tests/state.test.ts to spy on `FileHandle.prototype.sync` instead of `fs.fsyncSync`, then drop the redundant `fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd)` call. The cycle-2 spy is now testing the wrong abstraction layer.
evidence:
// Redundant but required for T-061's fs.fsync* spy ordering assertion.
    fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd);

title: checkManifest fetch has no timeout — blocked port can hang the probe
description: `fetch(MANIFEST_URL)` is called with no AbortSignal and no `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(N)`. The contract for probe #7 says 'short connect timeout'. On a healthy machine a refused localhost connection rejects fast (good), but on a host where port 9009 is bound by something hung (e.g. a stuck deploy from a previous run, a misconfigured docker-proxy holding the port open without responding), the fetch will hang for the OS default — Node's undici defaults can be tens of seconds. Combined with the post-deploy re-probe loop (3 attempts), that's potentially 3 × default_fetch_timeout of hang per remediation.
impact: Probe latency unbounded; under E-007-adjacent conditions the MCP request can stall the skill for an unbounded time. The deploy executor's 7-minute timeout doesn't cover the post-spawn re-probe phase since each fetch is independent.
recommendation: Use `fetch(MANIFEST_URL, { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(5000) })` (Node 20.3+) or a manual AbortController. Same hardening on each of the three retry calls. The catch block already maps any rejection to `false`, so adding a timeout is non-breaking.

title: runRealDeploy stdout/stderr handlers split chunks naively, splitting log lines that straddle reads
description: Each `data` event runs `chunk.toString().split('\n').filter(l => l.length > 0)` and pushes results into `logs`. There's no buffer for incomplete lines: if a Buffer arrives containing 'Error: cou' and the next contains 'ld not connect to localhost:9009\n', logs will contain two entries 'Error: cou' and 'ld not connect to localhost:9009', neither of which greps cleanly. The contract test T-144 sends pre-formed lines so it doesn't catch this; real pnpm deploy output absolutely does straddle chunk boundaries.
impact: Captured logs in the response are unreliable for downstream pattern matching. T-155's 'pnpm down' message generation depends on the logs being legible to the consolidator. Diagnostic noise rather than data loss.
recommendation: Maintain a small per-stream buffer: keep the trailing partial line, prepend it to the next chunk, and only push completed lines. Flush the residual buffer on close.

title: suiPilot probe swallows JSON parse error and reports it as 'plugin not enabled'
description: When `~/.claude/settings.json` exists but contains invalid JSON, the probe's catch handler returns the same 'sui-pilot plugin is not enabled. Run: claude plugins enable sui-pilot' message it returns when settings.json is missing or when sui-pilot is genuinely disabled. The three failure modes are diagnostically distinct: missing file (run `claude plugins enable sui-pilot`), parse error (fix the JSON syntax), and plugin disabled (run the command). Conflating them causes the student to run `claude plugins enable sui-pilot` against a broken settings.json, which will likely fail or — depending on the claude CLI's resilience — silently succeed and reformat away other unrelated state.
impact: Misleading remediation message in the rare-but-real 'corrupt settings.json' case. Doesn't match the cycle-1 outputStyle.ts pattern, which uses dedicated `settings-file-missing` vs `settings-parse-error` warning kinds (already exported from warnings.ts but unused by the new probe).
recommendation: Distinguish the three branches: ENOENT → 'settings file not found'; JSON parse error → 'settings.json has invalid JSON: <message>'; plugin not in enabledPlugins → current message. The PreflightFailWarning kind in warnings.ts can carry a probeId, but a richer surface or reusing SettingsParseErrorWarning would be cleaner.

title: withSandboxRepoAbsent is a documented no-op — silently succeeds when caller forgets to set HOME
description: The fixture's body is a comment block stating the probe reads HOME directly so the fixture is 'effectively a no-op'. If a future test calls `withSandboxRepoAbsent()` without first setting `process.env.HOME` to a temp directory, the fixture silently passes — and probeSandboxRepoPresent runs against the developer's real `~/workspace/deepbook-sandbox/`, which on the maintainer's machine almost certainly exists and returns pass:true. The test asserts pass:false, fails noisily on CI, but locally the developer sees a green run because their real sandbox checkout is present. False-positive test results during local iteration.
impact: Test scenario reliability; only matters when HOME is misconfigured by the caller. Cycle 3 tests do set HOME correctly today, so the harness fix doesn't manifest yet — but the fixture's contract is 'this fixture makes the sandbox absent', not 'this fixture asserts the caller already arranged absence'.
recommendation: Either (a) actively set HOME inside the fixture to a tmp directory it owns and unsets in shutdown, mirroring withDeployStub's lifecycle; or (b) assert/verify HOME points to a directory without `workspace/deepbook-sandbox/` and throw if not. Silent no-ops are a class of fallback hazard.
evidence: // fixture is effectively a no-op — but it exists for API completeness

title: runStubDeploy ignores withDeployStub options exitCode and exposeManifest
description: The harness's `withDeployStub({ exitCode, exposeManifest })` accepts these inputs but they're never plumbed through. `runStubDeploy()` returns hardcoded `{ pass: false, message: '[stub] deploy-all ran but manifest is not up. Run: pnpm down to clean up.', logs: [...] }` — the harness fixture's `exposeManifest: true` (used to model the happy E-008-stub path) silently has no effect, and `exitCode: 1` cannot be exercised at all. T-155 happens to want pass:false, so cycle-3 tests stay green; future scenarios wanting a stubbed pass:true path are blocked.
impact: Tests that set exposeManifest:true silently get the manifest-not-up branch — a quiet contract mismatch between the harness API surface and the actual stub. Not a runtime error today; a foot-gun for cycle 4+ scenario authors.
recommendation: Either thread the options through (e.g. read them from a module-level slot the harness sets via withDeployStub, or accept them as a parameter to runStubDeploy that the executor passes from a context object) or drop the unused parameters from the harness fixture signature. The current shape pretends to be configurable.

title: runRealDeploy timeout uses SIGTERM only — child ignoring SIGTERM hangs the executor indefinitely
description: On timeout, `child.kill()` (default SIGTERM) is called and the executor relies on the close event firing. A child that traps or ignores SIGTERM (pnpm itself doesn't, but a misbehaving deploy script run by pnpm could) won't exit, close won't fire, and the promise never resolves. Combined with R3-001 (no error handler), the failure modes for the deploy executor 'never resolve' are: spawn error before exec, child traps SIGTERM, child closes stdio without emitting close (rare but real on detached children).
impact: Long-tail hangs in the 7-minute-and-a-second class. Less likely than R3-001 in practice but worth tightening since the cost is small.
recommendation: Schedule a SIGKILL escalation a few seconds after the SIGTERM: `setTimeout(() => { try { child.kill('SIGKILL'); } catch {} }, 5000)`. Track whether close has fired and clear that timer in the close handler.
